observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to:
Direct the MT to strictly comply with the daily remittance and prompt reporting of all collections from issued AFs, as required by PD No. 1445, RA No. 7160, and the MNGAS for LGUs; and
Require that the monthly reconciliation of AFs be conducted using the Report of Accountability for AFs (RAAF) and Consolidated RAAF (CRAAF) and ensure that any discrepancies are promptly flagged and reported directly for appropriate action.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Require the MT to deposit daily and intact all collections to prevent the risk of its possible loss and/or misuse.;
Strictly implement the provisions governing collections and deposits under PD No. 1445, Section 313 of RA No. 7160 and MNGAS for LGUs; and
Compel the MT to deposit immediately the undeposited collections amounting to ₱3,225,114.44.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendations and Management agreed to:
Demand from the concerned AOs the immediate submission of liquidation documents and/or refund of the CAs if not yet utilized, otherwise, institute appropriate criminal or administrative sanctions for the continued non-compliance with the existing laws, rules and regulations on the granting, utilization and liquidation of CAs, if warranted;
Stop the practice of granting additional CAs to AOs with unliquidated CAs and transferring CAs from one AO to another in accordance with Sections 89 and 4.1.6 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular 97-002, respectively; and
Increase the bonding requirements of AOs with insufficient bond pursuant to Treasury Circular No. 02-2009 to protect the interest of the MGM, otherwise ensure that CA granted is within the limit of the fidelity bond.
Moreover, we recommended and the MA agreed to ensure the completeness of documents attached to the DVs prior to submission to COA to avoid suspensions or disallowances in audit.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the MA to:
Ensure proper accounting of inter-agency and intra-agency fund transfers through accurate recording of transactions and proper maintenance of GLs, SLs and supporting schedules that will serve as basis in the subsequent reporting periods;
Make the necessary adjustments to reclassify the Due to NGAs account into the Trust Liabilities account in accordance with Annex B of COA Circular No. 2015-009;
Review and analyze transactions affecting the fund transfers accounts thoroughly before taking them up in the books of accounts to ensure that they are properly recorded under the appropriate account in order to prevent misclassifications, abnormal balances and variances between the reciprocal accounts, and come up with accurate balances presented in the FS; and
Exert utmost effort in retrieving the necessary documents to support the accuracy and validity of the balances recorded under the fund transfers accounts, and thereafter, request for the write-off of dormant unliquidated fund transfers in accordance with COA Circular No. 2023-008, if warranted.
Likewise, we recommended and Management agreed to instruct the MA and the MT to:
Prioritize the reconciliation of its records with the SAs to facilitate the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts and in the cashbook, if warranted, to arrive at a correct/reconciled balance of the Due to NGAs accounts; and
Facilitate the prompt and proper submission of liquidation reports to SAs in accordance with the terms and conditions stipulated in the Memorandum of Agreement (MOA) or the provisions under COA Circular No. 94-013, in case no reportorial requirements are provided in the MOA.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Fast track the implementation of the programs/projects for which the funds received were intended in order to maximize the benefits that could be derived therefrom; and
Immediately return to the SAs any unutilized balances in accordance with the Memorandum of Agreement, if any, or Section 6 (7) of COA Circular No. 94-013.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
On unimplemented projects due to limited fund releases-
Direct the MT in ensuring the full and timely transfer of the 20% DF to its designated bank account; and
Establish a project monitoring and accountability framework to track physical and financial accomplishments, and to facilitate early identification of implementation issues for appropriate action.
On inadequate cash backing for obligated and unobligated funds-
Instruct the LFC to prepare a comprehensive cash flow plan aligned with the Annual Investment Plan, and to ensure actual collections in supporting programmed disbursements; and
Initiate timely fund realignments through proper legislative authority when specific projects are no longer viable or implementable within the current fiscal year.
On poorly monitored fuel consumption (Gasoline)-
Require the MGSO to implement a comprehensive vehicle monitoring system that includes trip tickets, fuel logs, and monthly fuel consumption summaries validated against actual activity reports; and
Establish fuel consumption benchmarks per vehicle based on historical usage and current operational needs to promote economy and efficiency.
On undocumented or untracked use of the backhoe-
Require the Municipal Engineer or assigned personnel to prepare and maintain detailed equipment utilization logs showing date, location, operator, project name, duration of use, and purpose; and
Ensure all backhoe use is based on approved job requests or work orders and that usage is acknowledged by the end-user or project supervisor
On livelihood financial assistance program deficiencies-
Instruct members of the MLGU-PMT and the PESO-designate to develop comprehensive livelihood project proposals, which must include:
Clear beneficiary identification and validation procedures;
Business plans or financial feasibility studies; and
Monitoring and evaluation tools and processes.
Establish a Livelihood Assistance Committee tasked with overseeing implementation, monitoring outcomes, and conducting post-disbursement verification visits to ensure fund utilization aligns with intended purposes.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation and Management agreed, upon authorization from the SB, to open a separate bank account in the GF for the current appropriations under the LDRRMF, and in the TF for the unexpended LDRRMF transferred to the STF to prevent comingling of funds and incurring cash deficit, and to closely monitor the disbursements charged to the LDRRMF.
We recommended and the Management agreed to:
Direct the MT to facilitate the immediate transfer of unspent DRRMF from the GF to the STF and thereafter, require the MA to properly take up the transfer in the books of the Municipality; and
Require the MDRRMO and the MA to prepare and submit the Report on Sources and Utilization of DRRMF on a monthly basis to the AT within the prescribed period.
Likewise, we recommended and the MA agreed to reclassify the Watercrafts account balance of ₱100,900.00 to Disaster Response and Rescue Equipment account.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Prioritize the filling up of vacant Plantilla positions and resort to hiring of casual or emergency staff in accordance with Section 2, Rule XI of CSC MC No. 40, series of 1998 and Section 211(b) of the GAAM, Volume I, in order to avoid hiring significant number of JO personnel; and
Facilitate the submission of copies of JO contracts executed by the MGM within the prescribed period under COA Circular No. 2009-001.
Likewise, we recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the MHRMO to:
Prioritize the formulation of a “Qualification Standards and Salary Rates for JO Positions” to come up with a HRP to be submitted to the SB for subsequent enactment of a resolution adopting the same;
Issue a uniform policy guideline on the hiring of JO personnel to properly determine the necessity of hiring JOs and to strengthen internal controls pertaining thereto; and
Prepare the JO contracts for the hiring of necessary JO personnel and indicate therein the specific duties, functions and deliverables expected from them as basis in evaluating their accomplishment reports and the propriety of payments to them, pursuant to existing laws, rules and regulations.
Also, we recommended and Management agreed to direct the MAO to ensure the completeness of supporting documents attached to the DVs, such as copies of JO contracts for initial payment of wages of JO personnel, in order to establish the propriety of expenditures, pursuant to Section 4(6) of PD No. 1445 and Section 1.1.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-001 and Section 4.1 of COA Circular No. 2012-003.
Further, we recommended and the concerned Committee of the SB agreed to take appropriate action to address all matters pertaining to the organization and management; personnel administration, position classification and pay plan, staffing patterns; and creation of positions and policy formulation, in connection with the observations noted in audit.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Submit to the AT the required certification from the Municipal Mayor for the aforementioned traveling expenses, otherwise, these may be suspended in audit; and
Strictly comply with the documentary requirements provided in existing laws, rules and regulations in granting claims for travel expenses of JO personnel in order to facilitate the prudent use of limited government funds.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GFP to prepare and submit a supplemental or revised GPB to the DILG Provincial Office whenever there are realignments or newly implemented GAD-related PPAs not included in the approved plan.  This will ensure proper documentation, effective monitoring, and compliance with relevant JMC provisions.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the GFPS to undertake the following measures:
Conduct gender analysis using available sex-disaggregated data to clearly identify and substantiate priority gender issues affecting the Municipality;
Capacitate GFPS members on the proper use and application of GAD tools such as the HGDG, PEMMI, and FEMMI, to ensure that PPAs are responsive and appropriately attributed; and
Integrate the results of HGDG assessments into the GPB and GAD AR to enhance transparency, monitoring, and evaluation of GAD-related interventions.

We reiterated the following prior year’s recommendation and Management agreed to require the:
MA to facilitate the reconciliation of prior year’s withheld taxes with the amounts remitted to determine the actual amounts due to BIR.  Thereafter, prepare the necessary adjusting entry to correct the Due to BIR account;
MT and MA to ensure availability of funds for the immediate remittance of the mandatory contributions and loan amortizations to GSIS, Pag-IBIG and PhilHealth to avoid paying penalties, surcharges and interest arising from delayed remittances; and
Require the liable/AOs to settle personally the interest due to GSIS and future penalties and surcharges if determined negligent in the performance of duties.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the LCE direct the MT to rigorously monitor and enforce compliance with remittance regulations and implement the following directives for revenue collectors:

Ensure prompt remittance of collections in adherence to the prescribed remittance schedule; and

Mandate the preparation of the Report of Collection and Deposit (RCD) upon each collection remittance, highlighting the actual date of remittance to the MT’s Office (MTO).

MT to always put the date of acceptance of the amount remitted by the collectors in the RCD for reference purposes;

Collectors, through the MT, to maintain updated cashbooks to monitor properly their collections and issuance of Accountable Forms (AFs); and

MT to monitor and enforce strictly daily remittance of collections by revenue collectors to avoid accumulation of cash and possible loss thereof through theft or misappropriation.

MT to deposit all collections intact daily or not later than the next banking day to the Authorized Government Depository Bank pursuant to the pertinent regulations on the receipts and deposit of government monies; 

Previous MTs to turn-over immediately their undeposited collections to the incumbent MT; and

Exhaust all administrative remedies to collect the undeposited collections or resort to legal sanctions, if warranted under the circumstances.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

Management should make a realistic income projection in the ensuing budget year and use the historical data as the basis thereof;

The Municipal Accountant, Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Treasurer closely coordinate with each other to ensure that no cash overdraft and cash deficit will be incurred at any given time of the year; and

observations.reason: This audit observation and the given recommendations were reiterated in Part II of this Report

observations.recommendation: We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the MA and MT to reconcile their respective records, identify the discrepancies and effect adjustments in the books, if warranted.  Also, they should reconcile their records regularly in accordance with the aforementioned regulation.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MA to cancel the stale checks and prepare the necessary adjusting entries to correct the Cash in Bank-LCCA account and the corresponding Payable accounts in the MGM’s books.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the Local Finance Committee to analyze the trend in the collection of tax revenues for the last five years and determine the amount of income that will likely be collected and use the data as inputs or benchmark in the preparation of Annual Budget for the ensuing fiscal year.
We also recommended and the Municipal Budget Officer, MA and the MT agreed to exercise diligence, prudence and proper control in the disposition of the MGM’s funds and limit expenditures within the total income realized and collected during the year to avoid cash deficiency.
We also recommended and the LCE agreed to require the MT to prepare the Cash Analysis Report monthly to monitor the cash position of the MGM and submit this to all concerned offices/departments to serve as parameter in settling obligations and ensure the stability of the MGM’s financial resources.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation and the MT and the MA agreed to immediately submit to the Office of the Auditor the subject DVs, checks and other supporting documents pertaining to CYs 2016 to 2020 transactions to determine the validity of these transactions.
We also recommended and the Management agreed to initiate administrative disciplinary action provided in Section 41(3) of PD No. 1445 to concerned officials and employees for any unjustified failure in rendering the un-submitted accounts.  We further recommended and the concerned Municipal officials agreed to submit within 15 calendar days the DVs and supporting documents otherwise, the AT shall be constrained to suspend payments made for the said un-submitted transactions or disallow payments without DVs.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the MT and the MA to submit on time the subject accounts and financial reports including the supporting documents.  We also recommended and Management agreed to monitor properly such activities so that reports are submitted promptly in accordance with Sections 100, 107 and 122 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the MA to confirm all outstanding balances of fund transfers under the Due to NGAs account and reconcile the latter with the SAs’ records.  Also, request formally from the SAs if the reconciled outstanding fund balances of completed PPAs could still be utilized by the MGM to finance other related PPAs.  Otherwise, we recommended and the LCE agreed to direct the MA and the MT to return immediately to the concerned SAs the unutilized fund balances.
We also recommended and the MA agreed to revisit the prior year’s accounting entries pertaining to GF transactions on fund transfers, and identify the erroneous entries that caused the abnormal/negative balance.  Thereafter, effect the necessary adjustments in the books of accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to ensure that the identified priority PPAs are implemented as planned and to utilize fully the LDRRMF within the scheduled timeframes.  In such manner, the in-house capability of the LGU to manage efficiently all types of emergencies and to achieve orderly transitions from response to sustained recovery in case of calamity will be strengthened.

We commended Management’s efforts in ensuring the gender-responsiveness, correctness and alignment of the entries contained in its GPB by obtaining the certificate of review and endorsement from the DILG Sorsogon Provincial Office.
On the other hand, we recommended and the GFPS agreed to:
Ensure timely submission of GAD-related reports to the AT in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular 2014-001; and
Maintain compliance with the provisions of PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC Nos. 2013-01 and 2016-01 on the preparation and submission of GAD-related reports.

We appreciated that Management had commenced the gathering of sex-disaggregated data to fully establish the GAD database.  However, review of available data revealed that the same was still insufficient to support GAD issues indicated in the GPB.
GAD database aids in surfacing gender gaps and/or differences among women and men in terms of their access to basic services and facilities, control of resources and enjoyment of rights, privileges, benefits and rewards thus, making it vital in identifying gender issues in order to formulate a gender-responsive plan.
The inability of the MGM to set up complete GAD database might have set aside other priority gender issues that could have been addressed immediately.
We commended Management’s efforts in integrating GAD in its policy-making through the enactment of a GAD Code.

Establish a complete GAD database by gathering other sex-disaggregated data, placing priority on the data relevant to the GAD issues indicated in the GPB to ensure that said issues represent actual gender gaps and/or differences among the constituents of the Municipality; and

We recommended that Management hire or designate General Services
Officer (GSO) who will be in charge in the safekeeping of LGUs assets together with
its records. Also, the inventory committee should conduct physical count of all PPE
every year to ensure the existence of the recorded PPE of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive initiate the conduct of
investigation on how the heavy equipment was lost and take the necessary
appropriate action or legal action, if warranted, in order to recover the subject
property.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurers
Office/the designated Property Officer and the Municipal Accountant to maintain the
required property records to effectively identify and monitor all the PPE of the
Municipality. Also, Management should require the previous supply/property officer
to turn-over all the property records of the LGU to the new officer.

We recommended that Management conduct a complete physical count of all
the unserviceable properties, prepare the IIRUP and immediately dispose the said
properties in accordance with COA Circular No. 92-386 on the disposal of
unserviceable properties.

We recommended that Management secure insurance with the GSIS on all of
its insurable properties. Also, prepare an inventory report of the said properties and
submit the same to the Office of the Auditor to verify its correctness.

Accordingly, in the rule against deficit spending, it is not a good fiscal
administration to go beyond ones financial capacity to spend and the LGU may not enjoy
good credit standing which might imperil its applications for credit accommodations when
there is a need to finance vital projects or to meet urgent demand for basic services.

In view of the noted deficiencies we reiterate our previous years
recommendations as follows:

a.) the Municipal Budget Officer, Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Accountant to stop the practice of incurring obligations without corresponding
cash back-up to avoid possible legal implications in case they would be unable
to meet its obligations;

b.) the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal Accountant to carefully monitor
the cash availability and settle all obligations as they fall due and use the cash
flow analysis to ensure that current and trust liabilities are paid promptly;

¢.) the Municipal Treasurer to strictly enforce the collection of taxes to be able
to augment its scarce resources; and

d.) Management should base the LGUs budget base on its actual collections to
make it realizable and the disbursements should be limited only within the
financial capacity of the Municipality.

Moreover, the Municipal Accountant should ensure that all of the reported
amounts in the financial statements were adequately supported with complete
schedule and/or documentations.

We recommended that Management stop the practice of drawing checks in the
name of the LGUs Officials and Employees but instead draw check in the name of
the suppliers or contractor. Likewise, Management to strictly observe the provisions
of the procurement law.

We recommended that the Municipality refrain from hiring the barangay of
elective officials so as not to hamper their mandated duties, functions and
responsibilities in their respective Barangay as provided in the RA No. 7160.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive requires:
1. Management to direct the employees to properly fill up the Leave
Application Form and the concerned personnel in the HRMO verify the
accuracy of the information stated therein;

2. The Municipal Accountant should not process claims with incomplete
documentations and she should sign/certify the computation of TLB and
MLC in accordance with COA Circular No. 2012-001. Also, review all the
Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) prepared by her staff; and

3. The Municipal Accountant, HRMO and Municipal Budget Officer to
coordinate closely before processing the claims to attest the veracity and
validity of the claims to be paid.

We also recommended that the overpayments made to Ms.Clara O. Garrido,
Ms. Gina Detecio, Ms. Olivia G. Gumba and Mr. Pio Garlan in the amount of
P5,829.50, £3,734.77, P303.61 and P662.17, respectively, be refunded immediately.

We recommended that all the transactions of the Municipality should be
meticulously reviewed before effecting payments. If the transaction is found to have
lacking documents it should be returned immediately to the originating department
for compliance. Also, all procurements should be in accordance with the provisions of
RA No. 9184 and its Revised IRR.

We recommended to the Local Chief Executive to require the Bids and
Awards Comnmittee to strictly observe the prohibitory provision of Section 54.1 of the
applicable IRR of RA 9184 on splitting of procurement opportunities and to set up
public bidding as the default mode of all procurement of the LGU, taking fully into
consideration that resort to other alternative modes is more of an exception rather
than a general rule.

We recommended that Management:

a. require the BAC, the Secretariat and the TWG to strictly abide by »the
standards set forth by RA No. 9184, its Revised IRR and other relevant
laws on government procurement;

b. require them also to exercise due diligence in the performance of their
duties to ensure that all requirements of the procurement law are faithfully
adhered to;

c. direct the Municipal Accountant to observe the requirements of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 and other pertinent regulations at all times to avoid
suspension or disallowance in audit; and

d. submit all the necessary supporting documents of the disbursement
vouchers which are not yet forwarded to COA.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and the Municipal
Accountant submit immediately to the Office of the Auditor the subject disbursement
vouchers, payrolls and checks pertaining to CY 2016 transactions to determine the
validity of these transactions.

We recommended that Management require the Municipal Treasurer and the
Municipal Accountant exert extra effort in preparing and submitting on time the
subject accounts and financial reports including the supporting documents. We
suggest that such activities be properly monitored so that reports are submitted
promptly in accordance with the pertinent provisions of PD No. 1445 and COA
Circular No. 2009-006.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Disaster
Risk Reduction and Management Officer to adhere to the provisions of the IRR of
RA 10121 and prepare LDRRMP, taking into consideration the legislated usage of
the fund in order that the necessary plans for risk reduction and proper utilization of
the fund can be achieved.

We recommended that the LCE require:

1. The Municipal Accountant to make sure that payments are supported with
complete documentation to ensure the validity, propriety, and legality of the
transactions; and

2. The LDRRMO to submit the Sanggunian Resolution declaring the state of
calamity including the results of damage assessment and needs analysis.

In relation with the noted deficiencies under this observation, we recommended that:

a. Management observe the requirements of NDRRMC-DBM-DILG Joint
b.

Memorandum Circular No. 2013-1 at all times to ensure that the LDRRMF
is used strictly for disaster risk reduction and management activities and to
warrant proper charges therefrom;

The Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant to enforce
that all payments for the procurement of goods and services be made by
check payable directly to suppliers/dealers/creditors in the disbursement of
government funds, except for petty disbursement out of petty cash fund;

c. LCE instruct the BAC to strictly comply with GPPB Resolution No. 09-
2009 dated November 23, 2009 specifically Item No. 3.a which states that
“the Request for Quotation (RFQ) shall indicate the specification,
quantity, Approved Budget for the Contract (ABC), and other terms and
condition of the item to be procured”; and

d. The Municipal Accountant should ensure that all disbursements are
supported with proper documentation prior to payment to avoid future
disallowance or suspension in audit.

We recommended that the Local School Board comply strictly with the
requirements of Section 272 of RA No. 7160 and DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular
Nos. 01 s. 1998, 01-A s. 2000 and 01-B s. 2001 and its amendments as follows:

a. identify the programs, projects and activities that reflect an equitable
distribution of the mandated priority projects and refrain from using the
SEF outside the scope for which it was established

b. Municipal Accountant strictly follow the allowed proper charges on the
SEF, likewise, refrain from certifying disbursements not related to SEF to
avoid possible issuance of COA audit suspension and/or disallowance; and

¢. Municipal Budget Officer assist the LSB in the proper charging of
appropriation during the preparation of the Local School Budget.

We recommended that the LSB maximize the implementation of the identified
and prioritized programs to ensure efficient utilization of the fund so that its
objectives are fully achieved.

We recommended that Management require the GAD Focal Person to:

a. Strictly observe the prescribed format and the timeline in the preparation
of the GAD Plan and Budget as provided in PCW/DILG/DBM/NEDA
Joint Memorandum Circular (JMC) No. 2013-01 to ensure timely
compliance thereof. Thus, GAD Plan and Budget should be prepared a
year before the budget for purposes of incorporating the GAD PPAs in
the Annual Investment Plan of the Municipality and in compliance with
the PPGD of CY 1995-2025; and

b. Prepare and maintain records on the detailed breakdown of expenditures
pertaining to GAD activities.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive requires:

a. The MPDO to spearhead the setting-up and maintenance of GAD
database; and

b. The GAD Focal Point System to ensure full compliance with the above
cited rules and regulations by regularly monitoring and expediting the
establishment of Local GAD Code.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the Municipal
Engineer, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer (MPDO) and other
concerned personnel to coordinate closely in monitoring the implementation of
projects funded from 20 per cent Development Fund to attain the optimal socio-
economic development and environmental management outcomes which is the core of
the fund allocation.

We reiterated our previous year’s recommendation that Management require the Inventory Committee to conduct physical count of all properties found in station and submit the corresponding Report of Physical Count of PPE and reconcile the same with the accounting records. Unserviceable properties and obsolete PPEs should also be reported in the Inventory Report for Unserviceable Property.

We recommended further that Management require the persdn responsible to prepare and submit the required reports pursuant to Section 111 of Presidential Decree No. 1445.
